title: Economic Policy Review: The Historical and Recent Behavior of Goods and Services Inflation

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: A 2004 Federal Reserve Bank of New York research paper by Peach, Rich, and Antoniades examines the historical relationship between services and goods inflation, finding that the then-record gap between these rates was likely to revert to its long-run average through services inflation moderating and goods inflation increasing. The study analyzes quarterly core PCE deflator data from 1967 to 2002.
key_points: The study covers quarterly core PCE deflator data from 1967 to 2002
Services prices have consistently increased faster than goods prices for decades
Since the late 1990s, the gap between services and goods inflation expanded to a record level
Over 35 years, services and goods inflation rates have tended to move in tandem with a reasonably stable difference
When the gap exceeds its long-run average, there is a tendency for mean reversion: services inflation slows while goods inflation increases
The authors argue the wide gap did not suggest imminent marked acceleration or dramatic slowing of overall U.S. inflation
why_it_matters: This historical research examined inflation dynamics and the mean-reverting behavior of the services-goods inflation gap, relevant to monetary policy discussions in 2004. However, the publication date (May 2004) is over 21 years before TODAY, raising questions about current archival value for market structure research.
macro_context: This research analyzed historical inflation patterns (1967-2002) and mean reversion dynamics between services and goods price inflation. It was published in 2004 when the Fed was monitoring inflation trends as part of its policy framework. The analysis has no current applicability given its age.
important_numbers: 35 years: historical period over which gap stability was analyzed

issues_found: Source published May 2004, over 21 years before TODAY (2026-04-10)

Record's own quality assessment flags lack of current relevance and staleness
Source content relates to macro context (inflation) rather than market structure as defined in requirements
